title: Knowledge Distillation
summary: Train smaller student to mimic larger teacher's soft output distributions. Temperature $T$ softens teacher outputs to reveal inter-class structure. Compresses the function, not the weights.
key_insights: Teacher's soft outputs (e.g., 'cat' 0.7, 'dog' 0.2, 'pet' 0.1) richer than hard labels ('cat')
Student trained on teacher's soft outputs via KL divergence + standard CE on hard labels
Temperature $T$ softens distribution: $\text{softmax}(z/T)$ — higher $T$ = more informative
Student architecture can differ completely from teacher
Compresses the function (input→output mapping), not the weight structure
Key for edge deployment and cost reduction

mathematical_core.temperature: $T > 1$ softens distributions, revealing inter-class relationships. $T = 1$: standard softmax.

derivations.title: Why soft labels carry more information than hard labels

steps.name: Hard label
steps.math: $y = [0, 0, 1, 0, \ldots]$ — one-hot. Information: $\log V$ bits (which class). No inter-class relationships.

steps.name: Soft label from teacher
steps.math: $P = [0.01, 0.05, 0.70, 0.15, 0.09, \ldots]$. Encodes: 'class 3, but class 4 is similar (0.15), class 1 is very different (0.01)'. Teacher's learned similarity structure.

steps.name: Information bound
steps.math: $H(P_{\text{soft}}) > 0 = H(\text{one-hot})$. Higher entropy = more information per example. Student learns from fewer examples.

derivations.consequence: Soft labels transfer the teacher's learned metric over the output space — not just 'what' but 'what else is similar'.

derivations.title: Temperature scaling — why $T^2$ appears in the loss

steps.name: Softmax with temperature
steps.math: $P_i = e^{z_i/T} / \sum_j e^{z_j/T}$. As $T \to \infty$: $P \to$ uniform. As $T \to 0$: $P \to$ one-hot.

steps.name: Gradient magnitudes
steps.math: $\partial D_{KL} / \partial z_{\text{student}}$ scales as $1/T^2$ (both softmaxes divided by $T$). Without compensation: distillation gradients vanish for large $T$.

steps.name: $T^2$ correction
steps.math: $$L = \alpha \cdot T^2 \cdot D_{KL}(P^T_{\text{teacher}} \| P^T_{\text{student}}) + (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{CE}(y, P^1_{\text{student}})$$ The $T^2$ exactly compensates the $1/T^2$ gradient shrinkage.

derivations.consequence: $T^2$ is gradient normalization, not arbitrary. Without it, the distillation signal becomes negligible for $T \gg 1$.

derivations.title: When distillation outperforms training from scratch
derivations.math: From scratch: $N$ examples $\times \log V$ bits each. Via distillation: $N$ examples $\times H(P_{\text{teacher}})$ bits. For $V = 50000$, teacher entropy $\approx 5$ bits: $5/16.6 \approx 0.3$ ratio. But those 5 bits encode similarity structure — more useful than class identity alone.
derivations.consequence: Most beneficial when: (1) teacher much larger than student, (2) large output space $V$, (3) classes have meaningful similarity structure.

geometric_properties: Teacher's soft labels encode a learned metric over the output space
Student's embedding space inherits teacher's similarity structure
Monitor: cosine similarity between teacher/student embeddings — should be high
statistical_properties: Temperature $T$ controls entropy of teaching signal
Typical $T$: 2–20. Higher = softer, more informative but noisier
$\alpha$ (mixing weight) typically 0.5–0.9 — more weight on distillation than hard labels
numerical_properties: Requires running teacher inference on all training data — one-time cost
Can pre-compute and cache teacher logits
Student training cost $\approx$ same as from scratch (same data, smaller model)
application_relevance: Distill large cloud model into task-specific smaller model at fraction of cost. Directly relevant to Anoop's local-first AI routing: distill cloud-quality into Ollama-sized models for specific tasks.
